content.text: What are Background Workers and Queues?

Background workers and message queues form the asynchronous backbone of production agent systems. Rather than executing a task inline and making the caller wait, the producer deposits a task description into a durable queue and returns immediately. Separate worker processes pull tasks from the queue, execute them — including all LLM calls, tool invocations, and multi-step reasoning loops — and write results to a store that the caller can poll or be notified about.

This producer/consumer separation decouples the submission of work from the execution of work. The queue acts as a buffer: it absorbs bursts of incoming tasks, preserves them durably across restarts, and delivers them to workers in a controlled order. Workers can run on entirely separate machines from the producers, scale independently, and restart without losing in-flight tasks — making this pattern the go-to architecture for long-running, resource-heavy, or bursty agent workloads.

. A task is a serialized unit of work — typically a JSON object describing what the agent should do (the user prompt, tool configuration, context) along with metadata like priority and retry budget. The queue holds tasks durably until a worker is ready. Workers are long-lived processes that poll or subscribe to the queue, lock a task, execute it, and report the outcome.

Agents place unique pressure on serving infrastructure. A single agent run can take anywhere from fifteen seconds to several minutes — far beyond the timeout thresholds of synchronous HTTP infrastructure (typical load balancers drop connections after 30–60 seconds). Running agents synchronously forces callers to hold open long-lived connections, which exhausts connection pools and makes the entire request path fragile.

Beyond latency, agents are computationally uneven. A document-analysis agent might complete in two seconds for a short memo and three minutes for a 400-page report. Synchronous serving must provision capacity for the worst case at all times; a queue-backed worker pool can absorb bursts into the queue and process them as capacity allows, dramatically improving resource utilization.

Durability is the third driver. If a synchronous agent server crashes mid-execution, the work is lost. Queues with at-least-once delivery semantics guarantee that tasks survive restarts: a worker that crashes before acknowledging a task returns it to the queue where another worker picks it up. This property is foundational for agent workflows that call expensive APIs or generate billable LLM tokens.

Producers and workers scale independently. During peak load the queue depth grows; the autoscaler adds workers. During off-peak, workers scale down. Producers never need to know about worker capacity.
Tasks survive crashes, deployments, and restarts. Queues like Amazon SQS and Redis Streams provide at-least-once delivery with configurable visibility timeouts so no task is silently dropped.
A spike of 10,000 agent requests can be enqueued instantly. Workers drain the queue at a steady rate, preventing the LLM API rate limits or downstream services from being hammered.

Failed tasks are automatically re-queued up to a configurable retry limit. Tasks that exhaust retries move to a dead-letter queue (DLQ) for inspection and manual replay rather than being silently discarded.
High-priority tasks (user-facing, time-sensitive) can be placed in a separate queue that workers drain first, while lower-priority batch jobs queue behind them.
Queue depth, worker throughput, task latency, and error rates are natural metrics. Instrumenting a queue-backed system is far simpler than instrumenting a tangle of inline async calls.
Producers can be Python web servers; workers can be Go processes. Any language that can serialize a JSON task and publish to the queue can participate.

The asynchronous model introduces complexity that synchronous architectures avoid. The client must now manage job state: it submits a task, receives a job ID, and must either poll a status endpoint or register a webhook to learn when the task completes. This round-trip is harder to implement on the client side than a simple HTTP request/response.
At-least-once delivery means tasks may execute more than once if a worker crashes after completing work but before acknowledging the queue. Worker handlers must therefore be

— executing the same task twice must produce the same result without side effects like double-charging a credit card or sending a duplicate notification. Designing idempotent agent handlers is non-trivial when agents have external side effects.

Ordering guarantees are weak by default. Standard queues offer FIFO ordering per shard but not across shards, and parallel workers destroy ordering guarantees entirely. Workflows where task B must consume the output of task A require explicit chaining logic (enqueue B after A completes) rather than natural request sequencing.
Running a queue system (Redis Cluster, RabbitMQ, SQS) adds infrastructure to manage, monitor, and back up.
Even a fast worker has a minimum latency equal to the queue publish + dequeue round-trip. For tasks that complete in under 500 ms, this overhead is significant relative to a direct synchronous call.

Tasks must be fully serializable as JSON (or another wire format). Large context payloads (e.g., multi-megabyte document uploads) should be stored in object storage with only a reference in the queue message.
Tracing a failure across producer → queue → worker → result store is harder than reading a single request log. Distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) is essential.
The lifecycle of a queued agent task has five phases: enqueue, delivery, execution, acknowledgment, and result retrieval.

The producer serializes a task payload — the agent's input, configuration, metadata, and a unique task ID — and publishes it to a named queue. The queue broker (Redis, RabbitMQ, SQS) accepts the message and stores it durably. The producer receives an acknowledgment from the broker and immediately returns a job ID to the caller.

Workers poll the queue or maintain a long-lived subscription. When a task is available, the broker delivers it to exactly one worker and marks it as "in-flight" (SQS calls this the visibility timeout). The message remains in the queue until the worker explicitly acknowledges it. If the worker crashes during processing, the visibility timeout expires and the message becomes visible to other workers.

The worker deserializes the task and runs the agent. For an LLM-backed agent this includes prompt construction, LLM API calls, tool dispatch, result parsing, and any further reasoning iterations. Long-running agent loops (ReAct, multi-agent orchestration) run entirely within the worker's execution context.
On successful completion the worker calls the broker's acknowledgment API (
in SQS). The broker removes the message. On failure the worker can either NACK (return the message to the queue immediately) or let the visibility timeout expire for implicit retry.
The worker writes the task result — the agent's output, status, error if any, and timing metadata — to a result store (Redis with TTL, PostgreSQL, S3 for large outputs). The caller polls a
endpoint or receives a webhook notification when the status transitions to
The retry logic is governed by a

. A common policy uses exponential backoff: the first retry is immediate, the second waits 30 seconds, the third waits 2 minutes, and subsequent retries double the interval up to a maximum. After exhausting the configured retry count the message moves to the dead-letter queue.

attrs.alt: Background Workers and Queue Architecture showing producers, message queue, worker pool, result store, DLQ, and autoscaler

content.text: The diagram shows the end-to-end flow. Producers (API servers, webhook handlers, scheduled triggers, or parent agents) enqueue tasks into the message queue. Workers dequeue tasks, execute the agent logic, and write results to the result store. The autoscaler monitors queue depth and adjusts the worker pool size. Failed tasks flow to the dead-letter queue after exhausting retries. Clients poll the result store via a job status endpoint or receive results through a webhook callback.
Background queues are the right choice whenever any of the following conditions hold:
Any agent that can take more than 30 seconds must be decoupled from the synchronous HTTP path. Queues are the standard solution.

If request volume spikes — for example, end-of-day batch processing, webhook storms from external services, or scheduled report generation — queues absorb the burst without overwhelming the worker pool.
When losing a task is unacceptable (e.g., processing a paid customer request, writing to a production database), the at-least-once delivery guarantee of a queue is essential.
Tasks that require sequential stages (retrieve → analyze → summarize → notify) can be modelled as chained queue tasks, where each stage enqueues the next on completion.
If some tasks (real-time user requests) must complete before others (background batch jobs) regardless of submission order, use separate queues with priority-aware workers.
The agent reliably completes in under 2–3 seconds and the caller genuinely needs to block for the result — synchronous HTTP is simpler and more debuggable.
The task volume is very low (a few tasks per hour) and infrastructure overhead is a concern — a simple in-process async task runner (asyncio, threading) may suffice.
Result ordering matters strictly and cannot be reconstructed from task metadata — queues make ordering hard to guarantee.
The following steps describe a production-ready queue-backed agent worker setup using Celery with Redis as the broker and result backend.
to your project. Ensure a Redis instance is accessible and configure the connection URL as an environment variable.
Create a Celery task that wraps your agent's entry point. The task receives the serialized input, invokes the agent, and returns the structured output. Mark it with
for access to retry logic via

Set concurrency (number of processes or threads per worker node), the queues to consume from, the prefetch multiplier (how many tasks a worker holds in memory at once), and the task time limit to prevent runaway agents.
In the API layer, replace direct agent invocation with a
ID and return it to the caller as a job ID.
route that looks up the Celery result by task ID, translates the state to a client-friendly schema, and returns status plus output when complete.
and a DLQ handler so failed tasks are routed to an inspection queue with an alert.
Configure Celery's built-in autoscaler or deploy KEDA (Kubernetes Event Driven Autoscaling) with a Redis queue depth scaler to spawn/kill worker pods based on queue length.
Enable OpenTelemetry tracing on the Celery worker with
so each task execution becomes a trace span correlated with the original HTTP request.

# dlq_handler.py — consume from the DLQ and alert
from celery import Celery
import os, json, logging

app = Celery(broker=os.environ["REDIS_URL"], backend=os.environ["REDIS_URL"])
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@app.task(name="tasks.handle_dlq_task")
def handle_dlq_task(task_name: str, task_args: list, task_kwargs: dict, error: str):
    """Called when a task exhausts its retry budget."""
    logger.error(
        "Task permanently failed",
        extra={
            "task_name": task_name,
            "kwargs": task_kwargs,
            "error": error,
        },
    )
    # Send alert to PagerDuty / Slack, write to audit DB, etc.
    send_alert(task_name, error)

def send_alert(task_name: str, error: str):
    # In production: POST to alerting webhook
    logger.critical(f"ALERT: {task_name} failed permanently: {error}")

# Wire the DLQ handler in app config:
# app.conf.task_reject_on_worker_lost = True
# app.conf.task_acks_late = True

Consider a legal-tech product that processes uploaded contracts: users submit a PDF, the agent extracts clauses, classifies risk, and generates a structured summary report. Processing a large contract takes 90 to 180 seconds — far too long for a synchronous HTTP flow — and the product receives bursts of uploads from law firms at the start of business hours.

The architecture uses a FastAPI upload endpoint that stores the PDF in S3 and enqueues a processing task with only the S3 key. A Celery worker pool running on GPU-less EC2 instances dequeues tasks, downloads the PDF from S3, calls a document-parsing service to extract text, then runs a multi-step agent: first an extraction pass (identify clause boundaries), then a classification pass (tag each clause by risk type), then a summary generation pass. The three LLM calls are made sequentially within the single task. Each pass uses a 200k-context model so the entire contract fits in one call per pass.

On failure (typically transient Anthropic API 529 overload errors), the worker retries with exponential backoff. After three failures, the task moves to the DLQ and an alert fires to the on-call engineer. The worker writes intermediate results to Redis after each pass so a partial recovery is possible — on retry it reads the Redis checkpoint and skips completed passes.

The queue depth peaks at around 800 tasks during morning load. KEDA monitors the Redis list length and scales the Celery worker deployment from 2 pods to 20 pods within 90 seconds. The firm's SLA is a 30-minute turnaround per document; the queue drains to zero within 25 minutes at peak scale.

The most dangerous mistake is writing a worker handler that performs non-idempotent operations without guards. If the worker crashes after sending an email but before acknowledging the queue message, the task will be retried and the email sent again. Every side effect (email sends, database writes, webhook calls, payment charges) must be guarded with an idempotency key.
Storing Large Payloads in the Queue Message

Message queues have strict size limits: SQS limits messages to 256 KB, Redis does not hard-limit but large messages degrade performance. Storing a full PDF or a 200k-token context in the queue message breaks these systems. The correct pattern is to store large data in object storage and put only the reference (S3 key, database row ID) in the queue message.

# WRONG: embed large document in the queue message
run_agent.delay(document_text=full_pdf_text)  # may be megabytes

# RIGHT: store in S3, pass reference
s3.put_object(Bucket="jobs", Key=f"inputs/{job_id}.txt", Body=full_pdf_text)
run_agent.delay(input_s3_key=f"inputs/{job_id}.txt", job_id=job_id)

An agent caught in a reasoning loop or blocked on a hung API call will hold a worker process indefinitely. Without a time limit the worker is starved from processing other tasks. Always configure both a soft time limit (raises a catchable exception for graceful shutdown) and a hard time limit (SIGKILL).
Acknowledge tasks only after successful completion. Combined with
, this ensures tasks are not lost when a worker is killed mid-execution.
means each worker process holds exactly one task in memory. This prevents a slow task from blocking other tasks queued behind it on the same worker process.
Emit OpenTelemetry spans at task start, each LLM call, and task end. Set the trace ID as a message attribute so client requests can be correlated to worker traces.
Size task payloads for the queue:
Keep queue messages under 10 KB. Reference large inputs via object storage keys. Return large outputs to S3 and put only the S3 key in the Celery result.
Never mix real-time user-facing tasks with long-running batch tasks in the same queue. Batch tasks starve real-time tasks when the queue is deep.
Monitor DLQ depth as an alert metric:
A non-zero DLQ is a production incident signal. Alert immediately; do not let DLQ items accumulate silently.
Use task IDs as idempotency keys:
Generate a stable task ID (UUID) on the producer side and pass it as a kwarg. Workers use this ID to gate side effects, making retries safe.
Inject artificial failures in staging — kill a worker mid-task, exhaust the retry budget, fill the DLQ — to validate that alerts fire and tasks can be replayed cleanly.

Queue throughput is measured in tasks per second (TPS). Celery with Redis can sustain several hundred TPS per worker node for lightweight tasks; agent tasks (dominated by LLM API latency of 2–30 seconds per call) typically achieve 0.1–10 TPS per worker process depending on concurrency and model latency. The bottleneck is almost never the queue itself — it is the LLM API rate limits and the number of worker processes.
Worker concurrency should match the I/O profile of the task. Agent tasks spend most of their time waiting on I/O (LLM API, tool calls, database queries). Use

as Celery's execution pool for agent tasks: a single process can run 100–1000 concurrent coroutines, all blocked on I/O. For CPU-bound tasks (image processing, local model inference) use the prefork pool (default) with concurrency equal to the number of CPU cores.

# I/O-bound agent workers: use gevent for high concurrency
celery -A tasks worker --pool=gevent --concurrency=200 -Q agent --loglevel=info

# CPU-bound workers (local model inference): prefork with core count
celery -A tasks worker --pool=prefork --concurrency=8 -Q inference --loglevel=info
Autoscaling with KEDA is the production-grade solution for Kubernetes deployments. Configure a
that reads the Redis list length and adjusts the worker replica count between a minimum (always-on warm workers) and a maximum (burst capacity).

# keda-scaledobject.yaml
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: agent-worker-scaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: agent-worker
  minReplicaCount: 2       # always-on warm workers
  maxReplicaCount: 50      # maximum burst capacity
  cooldownPeriod: 120      # seconds before scaling down
  triggers:
    - type: redis
      metadata:
        address: redis:6379
        listName: celery   # Celery's default Redis list key
        listLength: "10"   # target: 10 tasks per worker replica

The queue depth divided by the worker throughput gives the expected drain time. If queue depth is 500 tasks, each task takes 20 seconds, and there are 20 workers, the drain time is 500 / (20 * (1 / 20)) = 500 seconds. This simple model guides autoscaler configuration: if the SLA requires 10-minute drain time, set the worker count to satisfy queue_depth / (sla_seconds * worker_tps).
Most widely used; rich ecosystem; gevent/eventlet pools for async agents
Lighter than Celery; fewer features; good for low-complexity queuing
Cleaner API than Celery; actor model; fewer deployment patterns
First-class priority queues, rate limiting, repeatable jobs
Durable execution, built-in retry, versioned workflows; heavier ops
Serverless agent workers at AWS scale
No broker to manage; cold starts may delay short tasks
HTTP-based delivery; rate limiting built in
Reads queue depth from Redis/SQS/RabbitMQ to drive HPA
Long-running agents, burst workloads, retry-critical tasks
Agents completing in <5 s with strict result-wait requirements
Real-time token streaming for chat interfaces
Bursty, short-lived agents with unpredictable traffic
Periodic batch agents (nightly reports, refresh jobs)
Multi-step agents requiring explicit state management and versioning

Queue security starts with access control on the broker. Redis without authentication is a common vulnerability: any process with network access to the Redis port can enqueue arbitrary tasks and read all task results. In production, always configure Redis with
(AUTH) and TLS. For AWS SQS, use IAM policies to restrict which services can send and receive messages.

Task payloads should be treated as untrusted input. A compromised producer or a deserialization vulnerability can execute arbitrary code if Celery is configured to use pickle serialization. Always use JSON serialization (
) and validate all input fields before executing agent logic. Never deserialize complex Python objects from the queue message.

Result store access also requires control. Task results in Redis are readable by any process with broker credentials. If task results contain sensitive user data (LLM outputs, personal information, contract text), encrypt them at rest or store them in a secrets-aware result backend (dedicated PostgreSQL with row-level security, S3 with server-side encryption) rather than in the shared Redis instance.
No major queue system provides true exactly-once delivery without the application layer adding idempotency keys. Design every handler to be safe under duplicate execution.

A malformed task that consistently causes worker crashes (e.g., a corrupt PDF that crashes the parser) will cycle between the queue and the DLQ indefinitely unless a max-delivery-count limit is set. Always configure the DLQ and a maximum delivery count.

Set the visibility timeout (SQS) or task acknowledgment timeout (Celery acks_late) to be comfortably longer than the maximum expected task duration. A timeout shorter than the task duration causes duplicate delivery while the first execution is still running.
Workers must handle SIGTERM gracefully: finish the current task and then exit. Celery's
flags and a custom signal handler can ensure clean shutdown during rolling deployments.

If the LLM API returns 429 (rate limit) or 529 (overload), workers should not simply retry immediately — they will hammer the upstream service. Use a circuit breaker pattern: after N consecutive failures, stop accepting new tasks for a backoff period.

# Graceful SIGTERM handler for Celery workers
import signal, sys
from celery.signals import worker_shutdown

@worker_shutdown.connect
def on_worker_shutdown(**kwargs):
    # Flush any in-memory state, close DB connections, etc.
    print("Worker shutting down cleanly")
    sys.stdout.flush()

# In your systemd unit or Docker entrypoint:
# celery -A tasks worker --loglevel=info &
# trap "celery -A tasks control shutdown" SIGTERM SIGINT
Background workers and queues decouple task submission from execution, enabling agent workloads that run for seconds to minutes without tying up the HTTP request path.
At-least-once delivery guarantees durability but requires every worker handler to be idempotent — executing a task twice must produce the same result without duplicate side effects.
Queue depth is the primary autoscaling signal: use KEDA or Celery's autoscaler to spawn workers when the queue grows and retire them when it drains, matching capacity to demand.
Separate queues by SLA: isolate real-time user-facing tasks from long-running batch jobs so high-priority work is never blocked by a backlog of low-priority tasks.
Store only references in queue messages (S3 keys, database IDs), not large payloads; use checkpointing in the result store to make expensive multi-step agent tasks resumable after a worker crash.
The dead-letter queue is a first-class operational concern: alert on any non-zero DLQ depth, inspect failed tasks promptly, and build replay tooling before you need it in production.
For I/O-bound agent workers, use gevent or eventlet execution pools to run hundreds of concurrent LLM-blocked coroutines within a single worker process, dramatically improving throughput per machine.

Security requires TLS and authentication on the broker, JSON-only serialization (never pickle), encrypted result storage for sensitive outputs, and IAM or ACL-based access control on queue send/receive operations.
